mappings.rationale: Observed HSTS configuration can support verification that responses declare a one-year transport policy and, at higher assurance levels, include subdomains.
mappings.limitations: One response does not prove coverage across every route, host, subdomain, browser state, or the first unauthenticated HTTP visit.

mappings.rationale: HSTS is related to enforcing protected browser transport after the policy has been learned.
mappings.limitations: The header is not evidence that the broader transmission confidentiality and integrity control is fully implemented.

mappings.rationale: Observed CSP directives can support verification of trusted content loading and execution restrictions.
mappings.limitations: Header presence alone does not establish policy quality, route coverage, nonce correctness, or resistance to application-specific bypasses.

mappings.rationale: An explicit frame-ancestors directive can support verification of modern framing restrictions.
mappings.limitations: The auditor observes one response and does not prove that frame-ancestors is consistently deployed across every document response.

mappings.rationale: CSP is related to constraining executable browser content and reducing the privilege available to injected code.
mappings.limitations: CSP is a defense-in-depth mechanism and does not satisfy mobile-code control requirements by itself.

mappings.rationale: The nosniff directive directly supports verification that browsers are instructed not to guess response MIME types.
mappings.limitations: The header does not prove that the declared Content-Type is correct for the response body.

mappings.rationale: X-Frame-Options may provide legacy framing protection alongside the preferred CSP frame-ancestors directive.
mappings.limitations: ASVS identifies frame-ancestors as the required modern control and states that X-Frame-Options must not be relied upon alone.

mappings.rationale: An explicit restrictive referrer policy can support verification that URL data leakage is deliberately constrained.
mappings.limitations: One response cannot establish whether URLs contain sensitive data or whether every document applies the intended policy.

mappings.rationale: A valid CSP reporting directive linked to a trustworthy reporting endpoint can support verification that violation reporting is configured.
mappings.limitations: Configuration evidence does not prove browser delivery, collector availability, retention, triage, or incident response.

mappings.rationale: A recognized COOP directive can support verification that a document declares an intentional opener relationship policy.
mappings.limitations: The correct value depends on popup, payment, and federated sign-in requirements.

mappings.rationale: A restrictive CORP value can support verification that authenticated resources declare cross-origin loading boundaries.
mappings.limitations: The correct policy is resource-specific and cannot be inferred from the top-level response alone.

mappings.rationale: The bundle assesses whether the observed response declares the COOP and COEP values normally required for cross-origin isolation readiness.
mappings.limitations: Actual crossOriginIsolated state also depends on browser behavior and every dependent resource in the page graph.

mappings.rationale: CORP and compatible CORS behavior on dependent resources contribute to a coherent cross-origin isolation deployment.
mappings.limitations: A single-response header audit cannot inspect the complete dependency graph or runtime compatibility.
